role_summary: Baker County, Georgia homemaker turned civil rights activist; mother of Shirley Miller Sherrod (catalog entry 117); wife of Hosie Miller, whose 1965 murder by a white neighbor over a property dispute (acquitted by an all-white grand jury) anchored her family's commitment to civil rights organizing; later elected member of the Baker County Board of Education.
ai_reading: Grace Hall Miller's neighbor counts are moderate (peak 85), with her daughter Shirley Miller Sherrod (entry 117) as the top match and the broader cluster including Peggy Jean Connor (entry 96), the joint Geraldine Crawford Bennett / Toni Breaux / Willie Elliot Jenkins interview (entry 46), and Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones (entry 92). The mother-daughter cosine (Grace -> Shirley) is the catalog's clearest intergenerational pairing within a single family: both Grace and Shirley testify to the same 1965 murder of Hosie Miller from different vantage points (Grace as widow, Shirley as eldest daughter) and to the subsequent commitment of the family to civil rights organizing. The catalog flags a +0.73 Strategic Vision and +1.00 (MAX) Collective Discipline axis reading; for a Baker County homemaker who entered civil rights organizing through bereavement, the embedding's MAX Collective Discipline reading is the catalog's clearest case of a family-and-community-mediated organizing register reading as institutional discipline rather than as individual conscience.
biographical_paragraph: While Grace Hall Miller's LoC oral history carries a not-auditable audit tier (the catalog cannot independently certify the transcript against primary sources for this entry, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external archives before citation), the external biographical record is documented through the Civil Rights History Project finding aid for entry 50 [src: 1, src: 2]. Grace Hall Miller grew up in Baker County, Georgia and attended segregated schools there. She married Hosie Miller and they raised five children together, the eldest of whom is Shirley Miller Sherrod (catalog entry 117, the future USDA Georgia State Director of Rural Development) [src: 2]. In 1965 her husband Hosie was shot and killed by a white neighbor in a property dispute that an all-white Baker County grand jury declined to prosecute; Grace's commitment to civil rights organizing intensified after his death, and the Miller home in Newton became the headquarters of the Baker County Movement [src: 2]. Her children were among the African American students who integrated previously all-white Baker County schools across the late 1960s; the educational-integration experience of the family anchored Grace's later election to the Baker County Board of Education and her decades of advocacy for improved Black public education in southwest Georgia.
movement_context: Baker County, Georgia in the era of Grace Hall Miller's civil rights organizing was one of the southwest Georgia counties Sheriff L. Warren Johnson controlled through the late 1950s and into the 1960s under conditions that contemporary federal Department of Justice investigators and the Southern Regional Council documented as the "Bad Baker County" pattern of extrajudicial violence against Black residents and political subordination of the Black majority through registration suppression, peonage labor arrangements on the cotton and pecan plantations, and routinized sheriff-deputy beatings [src: 3]. The southwest Georgia organizing project that became the institutional context for the Miller family's post-1965 movement work was the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC) Southwest Georgia Project that Charles Sherrod (Grace Hall Miller's son-in-law, the husband of her daughter Shirley) opened in Albany in October 1961 with Cordell Reagon and Charles Jones; the project extended through Albany (Dougherty County), Terrell County, Lee County, Sumter County, Worth County, and Baker County, organizing voter-registration drives, citizenship-school classes, and the December 1961 to August 1962 Albany Movement mass actions through which approximately 1,000 Black residents of southwest Georgia were arrested over a sustained ten-month campaign [src: 3, 4]. The Miller home in Newton functioned through this period and through the late 1960s as the Baker County rural-headquarters extension of the SNCC Southwest Georgia Project, a pattern of rural-Black-landowning household movement infrastructure that the Charles Payne and Wesley Hogan organizing-tradition literature documents as the institutional substrate of the SNCC field-staff method [src: 4, 5]. The Hosie Miller killing in 1965 fits inside the documented southwest Georgia pattern of white-neighbor and white-employer violence against Black landowners and movement-affiliated families that the U.S. Department of Justice opened a Baker County civil rights investigation around in the same period; the all-white grand jury non-prosecution outcome the family experienced is the institutional pattern the federal investigators documented across the surrounding counties through the 1960s [src: 3]. The Baker County school-integration arc the Miller children participated in across the late 1960s ran alongside the broader southwest Georgia school-desegregation enforcement that followed the Green v. County School Board of New Kent County (1968) Supreme Court ruling requiring affirmative integration plans rather than freedom-of-choice schemes, and the subsequent Swann v. Charlotte-Mecklenburg Board of Education (1971) ruling affirming federal-court remedial authority over school-district desegregation [src: 6]. Grace Hall Miller's later election to the Baker County Board of Education sits at the institutional-electoral endpoint of the Voting Rights Act Section 5 enforcement and the southwest Georgia rural-school-board political incorporation arc the SNCC Southwest Georgia Project began organizing toward in 1961.
legacy_and_reception: The principal published reception of Grace Hall Miller's civil rights organizing runs through the documentary record of the Miller-Sherrod family and the SNCC Southwest Georgia Project rather than through a free-standing biography. Her daughter Shirley Sherrod's 2012 memoir The Courage to Hope: How I Stood Up to the Politics of Fear, written with Catherine Whitney and published by Atria, documents the Hosie Miller killing, the 1965 family decision to commit to civil rights organizing rather than leave Baker County, the integration of the Baker County schools by the Miller children, and Grace Hall Miller's subsequent election to the Baker County Board of Education from the perspective of the daughter who witnessed it [src: 7]. The Southwest Georgia Project for Community Education at Albany State University and the New Communities Inc. land trust at Cypress Pond, both founded by Charles and Shirley Sherrod, are the institutional continuations of the southwest Georgia movement infrastructure the Miller home in Newton was an early node of; New Communities Inc. was the first Community Land Trust in the United States (founded 1969 on 5,735 acres in Lee County), and it operated until 1985 when foreclosure followed by the 1997 Pigford v. Glickman class-action lawsuit settlement for USDA discriminatory loan denial returned a portion of the lost equity to the Sherrod family-led organization [src: 8]. The Civil Rights History Project oral history (Library of Congress entry 50 and Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture object record nmaahc_2011.174.67.1a-e) is the principal first-person record of Grace Hall Miller's own voice; the Smithsonian holding indicates institutional recognition of her testimony as part of the national civil rights documentary record [src: 1, 2].

gallery.alt: Baker County Courthouse in Newton, Baker County, Georgia, July 2017
gallery.caption: The Baker County Courthouse in Newton, Georgia, the county seat where the all-white grand jury that declined to prosecute the 1965 killing of Hosie Miller convened. Grace Hall Miller's home in Newton functioned as the Baker County rural-headquarters extension of the SNCC Southwest Georgia Project Charles Sherrod (her son-in-law) directed from Albany; her later election to the Baker County Board of Education sits at the institutional-electoral endpoint of the political incorporation arc the SNCC project began organizing toward in 1961.

gallery.alt: Baker County historical marker in Newton, Georgia
gallery.caption: The Baker County historical marker in Newton, Georgia. Baker County was one of the southwest Georgia counties that federal Department of Justice investigators and the Southern Regional Council documented through the early 1960s under the "Bad Baker County" pattern of extrajudicial violence against Black residents under Sheriff L. Warren Johnson; the SNCC Southwest Georgia Project that Charles Sherrod opened in Albany in October 1961 extended into Baker County and used the Miller home in Newton as its rural-headquarters node through the 1960s.

gallery.alt: Former Baker County High School building in Newton, Georgia
gallery.caption: The former Baker County High School in Newton, Georgia. The Miller children were among the African American students who integrated previously all-white Baker County schools across the late 1960s; the educational-integration experience of the family anchored Grace Hall Miller's subsequent election to the Baker County Board of Education and her decades of advocacy for improved Black public education in southwest Georgia.

sources.title: Stephen G. N. Tuck, Beyond Atlanta: The Struggle for Racial Equality in Georgia, 1940-1980
sources.publisher: University of Georgia Press, 2001; WorldCat OCLC 56068132 (the standard institutional history of the Georgia civil rights movement outside Atlanta, including the southwest Georgia counties of Albany, Terrell, Lee, Sumter, Worth, and Baker that the SNCC Southwest Georgia Project organized through the early 1960s, and the documentation of the "Bad Baker County" pattern of extrajudicial sheriff violence under L. Warren Johnson)

sources.title: Clayborne Carson, In Struggle: SNCC and the Black Awakening of the 1960s
sources.publisher: Harvard University Press, 1981 (paperback 1995); WorldCat OCLC 45283901 (the standard institutional history of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, including the SNCC Southwest Georgia Project Charles Sherrod opened in Albany in October 1961 with Cordell Reagon and Charles Jones, and the December 1961 to August 1962 Albany Movement)

sources.title: Charles M. Payne, I've Got the Light of Freedom: The Organizing Tradition and the Mississippi Freedom Struggle
sources.publisher: University of California Press, 1995 (second edition 2007); WorldCat OCLC 61664854 (the principal scholarly account of the SNCC organizing tradition and the rural-Black-landowning household movement-infrastructure pattern the Miller home in Newton was an example of within the southwest Georgia project)

sources.title: Wesley C. Hogan, Many Minds, One Heart: SNCC's Dream for a New America
sources.publisher: University of North Carolina Press, 2007 (paperback 2009); WorldCat OCLC 192129687 (institutional history of SNCC documenting the southwest Georgia field-staff network Charles Sherrod directed, the post-Voting-Rights-Act electoral incorporation work that produced Black-majority county school-board representation in counties like Baker, and the SNCC Legacy Project archive at the Atlanta University Center)

sources.title: Shirley Sherrod with Catherine Whitney, The Courage to Hope: How I Stood Up to the Politics of Fear
sources.publisher: Atria Books, 2012; WorldCat OCLC 779263430 (the memoir of Grace Hall Miller's daughter Shirley Miller Sherrod (catalog entry 117), documenting the 1965 killing of Hosie Miller, the family decision to commit to civil rights organizing in Baker County rather than leave, the integration of the Baker County schools by the Miller children, and Grace Hall Miller's subsequent election to the Baker County Board of Education)

sources.title: Monica M. White, Freedom Farmers: Agricultural Resistance and the Black Freedom Movement
sources.publisher: University of North Carolina Press, 2018; WorldCat OCLC 1031044156 (documents New Communities Inc., the first Community Land Trust in the United States, founded 1969 in Lee County, Georgia by Charles and Shirley Sherrod as the institutional continuation of the southwest Georgia movement infrastructure the Miller home in Newton was an early node of, and the Pigford v. Glickman 1997 USDA-discrimination class-action settlement context)

sources.publisher: Duke University in collaboration with the SNCC Legacy Project; the institutional archive entry for the SNCC Southwest Georgia Project Charles Sherrod opened in Albany in October 1961 and extended into Baker County through the 1960s
